cheatSheet:
# Design Patterns Quick Reference

## SOLID
- S: Single Responsibility
- O: Open/Closed
- L: Liskov Substitution
- I: Interface Segregation
- D: Dependency Inversion

## Creational: Singleton, Factory, Builder
## Structural: Adapter, Decorator, Facade, Proxy
## Behavioral: Observer, Strategy, Command
## Architectural: MVC, MVVM, Clean Architecture, Repository, CQRS

resources.title: Design Patterns: Elements of Reusable OO Software
resources.author: Gang of Four

plan.overview: Master the 15 essential design patterns every software engineer needs for interviews and production code.
plan.skippedTopics: Rare patterns like Flyweight, Memento, Visitor — they appear in <1% of interviews.

sessions.title: SOLID Principles
reviewQuestions: What's the difference between SRP and ISP?:::SRP is about classes having one reason to change -- it governs class design. ISP is about interfaces not forcing clients to depend on methods they don't use -- it governs interface design. A class can satisfy SRP (single responsibility) but still implement a fat interface that violates ISP. Example: a Printer class only handles printing (SRP satisfied), but if it implements a Machine interface with print(), scan(), fax() methods, clients that only need printing are forced to depend on scan() and fax() -- ISP violated.
sessions.successCriteria: Can identify and fix SOLID violations

sessions.title: Singleton Pattern
reviewQuestions: Why is Singleton considered an anti-pattern?:::Singleton introduces global mutable state, which makes code harder to reason about and test. It hides dependencies -- instead of declaring them in constructors, classes silently reach out to a global instance. It violates SRP because the class manages its own lifecycle AND its business logic. It makes unit testing painful because you cannot easily substitute a mock. Modern best practice: use a DI container to manage object lifecycle (Spring's singleton-scoped beans, for example). The DI container gives you singleton behavior without the anti-pattern drawbacks.

sessions.title: Factory Pattern

activities.description: Build a notification system with Factory

reviewQuestions: Simple vs Abstract Factory?:::Simple Factory creates one product type via a static method (e.g., NotificationFactory.create('email') returns an EmailNotification). Abstract Factory creates families of related products that must be consistent (e.g., DarkThemeFactory creates DarkButton + DarkInput + DarkModal as a coherent set). Simple Factory is a programming idiom; Abstract Factory is an official GoF pattern. Use Simple Factory when you just need to encapsulate creation logic. Use Abstract Factory when you have multiple related products that must be created together to maintain consistency.
sessions.successCriteria: Can implement Factory for real-world scenarios

sessions.title: Builder Pattern
reviewQuestions: When Builder over constructor?:::Use Builder when: (1) there are 4+ parameters, especially optional ones -- avoids telescoping constructors; (2) parameter ordering is error-prone -- Builder methods are named, self-documenting; (3) you need validation before construction -- the build() method can check invariants; (4) you want immutable objects -- Builder collects mutable state, then freezes it in the product. Avoid Builder for simple objects with 1-3 required parameters.

sessions.title: Observer Pattern
reviewQuestions: Observer vs Pub/Sub?:::In Observer, the subject directly maintains a list of observers and notifies them -- there is a direct reference between publisher and subscriber. In Pub/Sub, a message broker sits between them -- publishers emit events to the bus, subscribers listen on the bus, and neither knows about the other. Observer is synchronous and local. Pub/Sub can be asynchronous and distributed (think RabbitMQ, Kafka). Use Observer for in-process events. Use Pub/Sub for cross-service communication.
sessions.successCriteria: Can implement Observer with type safety

sessions.title: Strategy Pattern
reviewQuestions: Strategy vs if/else?:::Strategy eliminates if/else chains by turning each branch into a separate class implementing a common interface. Instead of `if (type == 'A') doA() else if (type == 'B') doB()`, you have `strategy.execute()` where strategy is an instance of StrategyA or StrategyB. Benefits: (1) Open/Closed -- add new strategies without modifying existing code; (2) Each strategy is independently testable; (3) Strategies can be swapped at runtime. The downside: more classes. For 2-3 simple cases, if/else may be clearer.

sessions.title: Decorator Pattern
reviewQuestions: Decorator vs inheritance?:::Decorator adds behavior at runtime by wrapping objects; inheritance adds at compile time by extending classes. Key differences: (1) Decorator is composable -- stack any combination in any order; inheritance creates a fixed hierarchy. (2) Decorator follows OCP -- add new behavior without modifying existing code. (3) Decorator avoids class explosion -- with N optional features, inheritance needs up to 2^N subclasses; Decorator needs N classes. (4) Decorator uses composition (has-a); inheritance uses is-a. Drawback: deep chains can be hard to debug.

sessions.title: Adapter Pattern
reviewQuestions: When Adapter vs Facade?:::Adapter converts one existing interface to match another expected interface -- it is a 1:1 translation wrapper. Facade provides a simplified interface to a complex subsystem of multiple classes -- it is a 1:many simplification. Use Adapter when you have an incompatible interface. Use Facade when you want to hide complexity. They can be combined: a facade might internally use adapters for legacy subsystems.

sessions.title: Facade Pattern
reviewQuestions: Facade vs Adapter?:::Facade provides a simplified interface to a complex subsystem -- it does not change interfaces, it simplifies them. Multiple subsystem classes are orchestrated behind one method. Adapter converts one existing interface to match another expected interface -- it translates, not simplifies. Facade intent: make it easier to use. Adapter intent: make it compatible. They can be combined: a Facade might use Adapters internally for legacy subsystems.

sessions.title: Command Pattern
reviewQuestions: Command vs Strategy?:::Command encapsulates a complete request as an object -- it stores the action, its parameters, and enough state to undo it. It answers WHAT to do. Strategy encapsulates an interchangeable algorithm -- it answers HOW to do something. Command has execute() + undo(); Strategy has a single method. Command instances are stored in histories/queues; Strategy instances are swapped in a context. Example: Command = 'delete character at position 5'. Strategy = 'sort using quicksort vs mergesort'.

sessions.title: Proxy Pattern

activities.description: Build caching proxy for API calls

reviewQuestions: Proxy vs Decorator?:::Both wrap an object implementing the same interface. The difference is intent. Proxy controls ACCESS: caching, auth, lazy loading. Decorator enhances BEHAVIOR: logging, retry, encryption. In code they look identical. The distinction matters for design communication. They can be stacked together.

sessions.title: MVC / MVVM / Clean Architecture

activities.description: Refactor a project to Clean Architecture

reviewQuestions: When Clean vs MVC?:::Use MVC for simple CRUD applications. Use Clean Architecture when: (1) Business logic is complex and needs independent testing; (2) Multiple delivery mechanisms exist (web, mobile, CLI); (3) The team is large and needs clear boundaries; (4) Requirements will evolve significantly. Clean Architecture costs more upfront but pays off in long-lived, complex systems. For a 5-endpoint CRUD API, Clean Architecture is overkill.
sessions.successCriteria: Can structure a project with Clean Architecture

sessions.title: Repository Pattern
reviewQuestions: Why not call the DB directly?:::Three problems: (1) Untestable -- need a running DB for every test. With Repository, inject InMemoryRepo. (2) Coupled -- changing from PostgreSQL to MongoDB means rewriting every service. Repository isolates the change. (3) Duplication -- same queries scattered across services. Repository centralizes. Repository follows DIP: business logic depends on an interface, not JDBC/ORM concretions.
sessions.successCriteria: Can implement Repository with in-memory and SQL backends

sessions.title: Event Sourcing & CQRS
reviewQuestions: When Event Sourcing over CRUD?:::Use Event Sourcing when: (1) Complete audit trail is legally required; (2) You need temporal queries -- 'what was the balance on March 15?'; (3) You need to debug by replaying events; (4) Multiple read models need different projections. Stick with CRUD when the domain is simple and history is not valuable. Event Sourcing adds overhead: event schema evolution, snapshot management, eventual consistency.
sessions.successCriteria: Can implement event store with state replay

sessions.title: Anti-Patterns & Code Smells

activities.description: Code review exercise: find and fix anti-patterns

reviewQuestions: Name 3 code smells:::Three critical code smells: (1) **God Object** -- a class handling auth, payment, email, reporting. Violates SRP. Fix: split into focused services. (2) **Long Parameter List** -- method with 5+ params. Fix: group into a dataclass or use Builder. (3) **Tight Coupling** -- directly instantiating dependencies. Violates DIP. Fix: depend on interfaces, inject via constructor. Other notable smells: duplicate code (extract function), feature envy (move method), magic numbers (named constants).
sessions.successCriteria: Can identify anti-patterns in any codebase

quizBank.question: Which SOLID principle does Factory support?
options: A) SRP
B) Open/Closed
C) LSP
D) DIP
quizBank.explanation: Factory lets you add new types without modifying existing code.

quizBank.question: Decorator vs inheritance?
quizBank.explanation: Decorator adds behavior at runtime without modifying the class. Inheritance is compile-time and creates tight coupling.

quizBank.question: When should you NOT use Singleton?
quizBank.explanation: When testability matters (global state is hard to mock), when multiple instances might be needed, or when DI is available.

quizBank.question: What is CQRS?
quizBank.explanation: Separate read and write models. Writes go through command handlers to an event store. Reads use optimized materialized views.

quizBank.question: Identify the anti-pattern: class ReportManager handles auth, data fetching, PDF generation, and email.
quizBank.explanation: God Object. Split into AuthService, DataService, ReportGenerator, EmailService.

quizBank.question: What is the key structural difference between Decorator and Proxy patterns?
options: A) Decorator wraps objects to add behavior; Proxy wraps objects to control access
B) Proxy adds new methods; Decorator only modifies existing ones
C) Decorator is used at compile time; Proxy is used at runtime
D) There is no difference — they are identical patterns
quizBank.explanation: Both patterns use a wrapper that implements the same interface as the wrapped object. The intent differs: Decorator enhances or extends behavior (adds logging, retry, caching to a method). Proxy controls access to the real object (authentication, lazy initialization, remote access). In practice, a caching proxy and a caching decorator look identical in code — the distinction is conceptual purpose. Use Decorator when the focus is feature enhancement; use Proxy when the focus is access control or lifecycle management.

quizBank.question: Which scenario is the Command pattern BEST suited for?
options: A) Choosing between sorting algorithms at runtime
B) Providing a simplified interface to a complex subsystem
C) Implementing undo/redo functionality in a text editor
D) Converting one interface to another for legacy integration
quizBank.explanation: Command encapsulates a request as an object with execute() and undo() methods. This enables undo/redo stacks (text editors, Photoshop), request queuing (job systems, task schedulers), and transaction logging (event sourcing). Choice A is Strategy. Choice B is Facade. Choice D is Adapter. The hallmark of Command is that the request itself becomes a first-class object that can be stored, queued, replayed, or reversed.

quizBank.question: In Clean Architecture, which layer is at the innermost core and has ZERO dependencies on outer layers?
options: A) Interface Adapters (Controllers, Presenters)
B) Frameworks & Drivers (Express, PostgreSQL, React)
C) Use Cases (Application Business Rules)
D) Entities (Enterprise Business Rules)
quizBank.explanation: Clean Architecture's Dependency Rule states that source code dependencies must always point inward. Entities are the innermost layer — pure business objects with no knowledge of databases, frameworks, or delivery mechanisms. Use Cases (application logic) depend only on Entities. Interface Adapters (controllers, repositories) depend on Use Cases. Frameworks & Drivers are the outermost layer. This makes Entities fully testable with zero infrastructure.

quizBank.question: A developer builds an OrderService that directly instantiates MySQLDatabase, SMTPEmailer, and StripePayment inside its methods. Which anti-pattern does this represent?
options: A) Golden Hammer
B) God Object
C) Tight Coupling / Dependency Inversion violation
D) Premature Optimization
quizBank.explanation: Directly instantiating concrete dependencies inside a class violates the Dependency Inversion Principle (DIP) — high-level modules should depend on abstractions, not concretions. OrderService becomes tightly coupled to MySQL, SMTP, and Stripe — untestable, unswappable, and fragile. The fix is Dependency Injection: accept IDatabase, IEmailer, IPayment interfaces in the constructor. God Object is one class doing too many unrelated things. Golden Hammer is using one tool for every problem.

quizBank.question: What is the key difference between Simple Factory and Abstract Factory?
options: A) Simple Factory uses static methods; Abstract Factory cannot
B) Abstract Factory creates families of related objects; Simple Factory creates one type of object
C) Simple Factory is a design pattern; Abstract Factory is just a programming technique
D) Abstract Factory always uses inheritance; Simple Factory uses composition
quizBank.explanation: Simple Factory: one factory creates one product type — e.g., NotificationFactory.create('email' | 'sms' | 'push'). Abstract Factory: creates families of related products — e.g., UIFactory creates Button + Input + Modal as a coherent set (DarkThemeFactory vs LightThemeFactory). Abstract Factory enforces consistency across related objects; Simple Factory just encapsulates creation logic. Abstract Factory is the formal GoF pattern; Simple Factory is a useful programming idiom, not an official GoF pattern.

interviewTips: Don't over-engineer — simplest pattern that works
Be ready to draw UML diagrams
Composition over inheritance — always mention this
